blurb: An exquisite xeric native of sandy ground: a fine green clump topped by an airy, see-through cloud of tiny seeds on hair-thin branches — and a historically important Indigenous grain.
water: Low; very xeric — thrives on sandy, dry, lean ground
seasons: Fine green clump in spring; delicate airy seed clouds in early summer; bleaches golden and goes summer-dormant in heat
wildlife: Seeds are important food for birds and small mammals; larval host; cool-season
toxic: Non-toxic; seeds were a staple Indigenous food grain

shots.cap: Early summer — delicate airy seed cloud · © Phyllis Holst (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — fine clump on dry ground · © Ryan Donnelly (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

care.hardiness: Very winter hardy with a broad climatic adaptation, found from 2,000 up to 10,000 ft elevation, so Boulder's zone 5b–6a cold and our intense high-altitude sun are no trouble.[1] It is a native, cool-season bunchgrass that does best in lean, dry, sandy ground and is one of the most drought-tolerant of the native range grasses.[1][3]
care.planting: A cool-season grass: it greens up and grows in early spring, sets seed by mid-summer, then bleaches and goes summer-dormant in the heat — so plant container stock or plugs out in early spring (after soil is workable) or in fall, when its growth cycle is active, not in the hot, dry June–September window NRCS specifically advises against for seeding.[1] In containers it absolutely must have a gritty, sharp-draining cactus-type mix and be kept on the dry side; it will rot in a moisture-retentive potting soil.[1]
care.sun: Full sun — it does well even on hot, dry, south-facing exposures, and resents shading.[1][2]
care.soil: Sharp drainage is essential. It prefers sandy, coarse-textured, well-drained soils and does NOT tolerate poorly drained ground, extended inundation, or winter-wet feet — a serious caution on Boulder's heavy alkaline clay, where it needs a raised, gritty, fast-draining bed rather than unamended clay.[1] It prefers neutral soils but tolerates weakly saline and sodic conditions and high calcium.[1][2]
care.water: Low; very xeric. It grows on as little as 6–14 in of annual precipitation and is exceptionally drought-tolerant, so once established it needs no supplemental water — and overwatering, especially in summer dormancy, is the fastest way to kill it.[1][3]
care.spacing: Set plants/plugs about 12 in apart; in seed-production culture rows are spaced 24–36 in.[1]
care.propagation: Reproduces by seed. Germination is notoriously slow and erratic because of a thick hull and embryo dormancy, and the seed is often hard — stands can take 2 to 5 years to fully establish.[1] Buying plugs or container plants is far more reliable for the home garden than starting from seed; division is impractical for this short-lived, seed-reproducing clumper.[2]
care.sow: Best sown by dormant fall seeding on light/sandy soils (which improves germination of dormant seed) or in very early spring on heavier soils; summer/late-summer (June–September) sowing is not recommended.[1]
care.stratify: The hard, dormant seed needs cold stratification to germinate; deeper planting and the natural cold-moist conditions of a dormant fall sowing help break dormancy, and acid scarification (or mechanical scarification) is often used to improve germination.[1][2]
care.depth: Sow about 1/2 to 1 in deep on medium-to-fine soils and 1 to 3 in deep on coarse sandy soils — the deeper placement on sand puts seed into moist soil to aid stratification and keeps rodents from digging it up.[1]
care.bloom: Flowers (yellow-green, on the airy see-through panicle) appear roughly June through September, with seed maturing by mid-summer.[2]
care.maintenance: Very low maintenance — cut the clump back to a few inches in late winter before new spring growth starts, and otherwise leave it alone. Avoid summer irrigation and rich feeding; it benefits from only light fertilization.[1] New stands may need weed control while they slowly fill in.[1]
care.selfsow: It is not weedy or invasive, but it does self-sow modestly by seed and is naturally an early colonizer of open, disturbed sandy sites; most plantings stay put and any spread is slow.[1]
care.troubles: Largely trouble-free where drainage is sharp; the real killers here are poorly drained or over-irrigated soil and summer wet during dormancy.[1] Grasshoppers may damage new stands.[1]

edible.summary: The nutritious seed was a staple food of many American Indian peoples, ground into meal and flour.[1][4]
edible.parts: The plump, protein-rich seeds — borne on the airy panicle and shattering readily when ripe in early-to-mid summer. They were one of the most important wild grains across the arid West.[1][3][4]
edible.preparation: Traditionally the seeds were parched, then ground into a fine meal or flour and made into mush, porridge, bread, cakes, or dumplings — often mixed with cornmeal; some peoples also kept it as a reserve food.[4][3]
edible.caution: Harvesting is laborious: the tiny seeds shatter from the heads as they ripen and must be gathered quickly and winnowed from the husk. As with any wild grass grain, inspect the seedheads and discard any showing the dark, hardened, ergot-like fungal bodies that can infect grass florets, and be sure of your identification before eating.[1][3]
